text: Increased peak hip adduction is a risk factor for patellofemoral pain in female runners.
Patellofemoral pain in runners is associated with increased peak hip adduction, increased hip internal rotation, and increased contralateral pelvic drop.
Reduced peak hip flexion during running is associated with patellofemoral pain.
Altered peak force and time to peak force at the foot are risk factors for patellofemoral pain development in runners.
Running gait retraining reduces pain and improves function in runners with patellofemoral pain, and significantly reduces peak hip adduction as a possible mechanism.
Proximal hip strengthening exercises improve pain and function in runners with patellofemoral pain but do not significantly reduce peak hip adduction during running.
Patellofemoral pain is the most prevalent running-related pathology.
